verses.text: He that separates himself seeks his own desire and rages against wisdom; a fool delights not in understanding but in airing his heart; with the wicked comes contempt and shame. Isolation breeds folly. The proud reveal their emptiness by loving only their own opinion.

He that separates himself seeks his own desire and rages against wisdom; a fool delights not in understanding but in airing his heart; with the wicked comes contempt and shame. Isolation breeds folly. The proud reveal their emptiness by loving only their own opinion.

He that separates himself seeks his own desire and rages against wisdom; a fool delights not in understanding but in airing his heart; with the wicked comes contempt and shame. Isolation breeds folly. The proud reveal their emptiness by loving only their own opinion.

The words of a man's mouth are as deep waters, and wisdom a flowing brook; it is not good to favour the wicked or wrong the just; 'a fool's lips enter into contention, and his mouth calleth for strokes.' Words run deep. The fool's talk invites the very trouble he reaps.

The words of a man's mouth are as deep waters, and wisdom a flowing brook; it is not good to favour the wicked or wrong the just; 'a fool's lips enter into contention, and his mouth calleth for strokes.' Words run deep. The fool's talk invites the very trouble he reaps.

The words of a man's mouth are as deep waters, and wisdom a flowing brook; it is not good to favour the wicked or wrong the just; 'a fool's lips enter into contention, and his mouth calleth for strokes.' Words run deep. The fool's talk invites the very trouble he reaps.

'A fool's mouth is his destruction,' his lips a snare to his soul; a talebearer's words are wounds that sink deep; 'he also that is slothful in his work is brother to him that is a great waster.' The tongue can destroy. Idleness ruins as surely as wastefulness.

'A fool's mouth is his destruction,' his lips a snare to his soul; a talebearer's words are wounds that sink deep; 'he also that is slothful in his work is brother to him that is a great waster.' The tongue can destroy. Idleness ruins as surely as wastefulness.

'The name of the LORD is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe'; the rich man's wealth is his imagined city; 'before destruction the heart of man is haughty, and before honour is humility.' God is the true refuge. Pride precedes a fall; humility precedes honour.

He that answers a matter before he hears it, it is folly and shame; a man's spirit sustains his sickness, but a wounded spirit who can bear? the heart of the prudent gets knowledge. Listen before judging. The wise keep learning, and the spirit bears what the body cannot.

A man's gift makes room for him before the great; the first to plead seems right till his neighbour searches him; the lot causes contentions to cease. Hear both sides. Truth often waits for the second voice and a fair settling.

A brother offended is harder to win than a strong city; a man is filled with the fruit of his mouth; 'death and life are in the power of the tongue,' and they that love it eat its fruit. The tongue is mighty. Our words bring forth life or death, and we taste their harvest.

'Whoso findeth a wife findeth a good thing, and obtaineth favour of the LORD'; the poor uses entreaties, the rich answers roughly; a man of many companions may be ruined, but 'there is a friend that sticketh closer than a brother.' A good wife is God's gift, and a true friend more faithful than kin.
